info.parenttitle: German and Italian Expansion (1933-1940) - Causes of expansion

markdown:
## The End of Collective Security


![Collective Security and the League of Nations](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/cf0835ae31cbf0794bc310db3d40531849a7967e-823x1113.jpg)

1. As we have seen in previous sections, **the 1930s was a period of heavy economic and political crisis, **impacting both on the domestic and foreign policies of nations.
2. The idea of the end of collective security shows how **WW2 was in a way allowed by the lack of commitment **of countries to preserve peace.

<callout type="note">* **"Collective security"** in the 1930s referred to the idea that **countries would act together to prevent aggression and maintain peace, typically through international cooperation**, especially within the framework of the **League of Nations.**
* It was an **ideal** that emerged after the traumatic consequences of WW1, but it proved to be no more than that.</callout>

## Why did collective security fail?


### **1. The Impact of the Great Depression**


1. The Great Depression brought **high levels of unemployment and very low productivity.**
2. The political translation this had was that peoples in different parts of the world grew **disillusioned by democracy and liberal capitalism.**
3. Partly as a consequence of the Great Depression,** Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933 **with an **explicit aim of disrupting international order and rejecting treaties.**
4. **The US had not joined the League of Nations in 1920**, and the Depression only deepened its **political isolationism**. 
  1. Apart from recalling loans, the Americans raised tariffs and established **protectionist** measures.
  2. **This suggested that the US would not be available to support or uphold international peace if a crisis erupted.**

<callout type="example">* In July 1933, **President Roosevelt rejected proposals **at the London World Economic Conference to collaborate on stabilizing international currency values. 
* Later, in 1935, the U.S. Senate approved a temporary **Neutrality Act,** which was expanded into a more comprehensive version in 1937.</callout>

<callout type="note">* The **growth of authoritarian regimes **as a response to the Great Depression did not happen only in Europe, it was a **global phenomenon.**
* In **Paper 2 - Topic 10: Authoritarian States, we will see the case study of Juan Perón in Argentina **as one of these examples in the Americas region.</callout>

### **2. The League of Nations Failed as a Peace Keeping Body**


1. **The League came into existence in January 1920,** as part of the results of the Paris Peace Conference in Versailles.
2. In 1920 there were **42 countries in the League**, and in 1933 (before Japan and Germany leave in that year), membership peaked to **60 countries.**
3. As an **aim**, it strove for **collective peace sustained by the goodwill and collaboration of member countries.** Nevertheless, it had initial **structural weaknesses:**
  1. The **Assembly** of the League had one representative from each country. 
      1. They could vote on general policies and budget, but for important decisions, such as the ones related to security, they needed a **unanimous vote.**
  2. This means that **decisions were difficult to achieve**, because if only one country refused a proposal, it would not pass.
  3. The **Security Council **was made up by **permanent and non permanent members. **
  4. The **first permanent members were Britain, France, Italy, and Japan,** and they all had **veto power.** 
      1. That meant that decisions over borders, potential military intervention and sanctions had to be fully agreed upon. Again, a **difficult task!**
  5. **Important countries were absent.** 
      1. Even when the League was the brainchild of Woodrow Wilson, the US president at the time,** the US Congress did not approve of the US joining the League**: they were adamant of focusing on isolationism.
4. The lack of support of the US impacted on the League both economically and symbolically. 
  1. Also, it was a flaw that two of the most powerful states in Europe,** Germany and Russia, were not members from the beginning.**
5. **The League did not have an army of its own**. 
  1. As a peacekeeping organization, the League relied on cooperation and common values and interests to foster diplomacy instead of military clash.
6. Nevertheless, articles 10-16 of the covenant of the League did explicitly state the concept of “collective security”: **if one country of the League got attacked by a third party, all the other members would join to protect the one being attacked.**
7. Also, article 16 stated that if **a member** **resorted to war** in violation of the Covenant, it would be considered an **act of war against all members**, and **economic and military sanctions** could be applied.
8. **During the 1920s the League was overall respected and its decisions abided by. **
  1. For example, they were able to sort out conflict in Upper Silesia, in the Aaland Islands and prevented hostilities between Greece and Bulgaria, among other successes.
9. **But in the 1930s, it had major failures. **
  1. **The inaction in Manchuria, as we have seen, undermined its credibility and prompted Japan to leave the League.**
10. When **Hitler** got to power, he** withdrew from the League **and in 1935-6, the international organization faced a **death blow in the conflict of Abyssinia** (see the section on Italian expansion).

<callout type="warning">The **League of Nations** is an institution created during the Peace settlement in Paris, but **it is not the same** as the Treaty of Versailles, which dealt with Germany after they lost WW1.</callout>



<callout type="self_review">* We have organized **the reasons for the end of collective security in two main blocs:**
  * It failed because the **Great Depression** made expansionist authoritarianism rise.
  * It failed because the **international organizations **meant to protect peace were not successful in deterring aggression.
* **Which one do you think is the most relevant?** 
* Does the **war start because it can’t be prevented? **
* Or because there are **aggressors that will wage war no matter what?**</callout>

<callout type="tip">This question can be tackled with an** intentionalist/structuralist historiographical outlook,** as we have seen for Hitler in the section of **Impact of Fascism and Nazism on the Foreign Policies of Italy and Germany. **</callout>

![League of Nations](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ea1c261377f5e7f8d5bfb04233b7a1262404c8c2-1600x900.jpg)
